title: From The Whole To A Part
study: A Study In The Life Of John The
scriptureForWeek: VARIOUS PASSAGES IN THE GOSPELS Make sure you focus on the verses for each day, as these important events are taken from several readings of scripture across several chapters. These are chosen to highlight the ministry of John the Baptist and to frame up the gap between the Old and New Testament timelines.
weekTheme: Boys React, Men Prepare God will use you. GOD IS SOVEREIGN, YET HE CHOOSES TO USE MEN TO ACCOMPLISH HIS WORK IN THE WORLD Have you ever been stuck in traffic when you hear an ambulance coming behind you? The sirens are blaring, the lights are flashing, and the horn is honking like crazy. There is an emergency and they need to get through. The driver is loud, urgent, and persistent. Sometimes the police will shut down traffic so they can get through. Other times the cars pull to the side and it’s like the parting of the Red Sea. A blocked road is bad news when someone needs to get through. In the same way, there are many things blocking God from working in our world today. God is looking for men to help clear the way so his message of love and hope can get through. John the Baptist was one of the men who cleared the way for Jesus. That’s why he was called a forerunner. He went ahead of him to make sure things were in place for Jesus to appear. He captured people’s attention, turned their hearts back to God, baptized them in repentance, and cleared a path through society. He was a siren and horn and flashing lights communicating someone important was on the way. You were not put on earth to twiddle your thumbs and amuse yourself until you get older and get a job. These years matter. God is wanting to show up in a fresh way, but the hearts of so many are blocked. He needs men willing to clear the way so his message can get through.

days.title: The Story That Really Matters
days.scripture: LUKE 3:1-6 It was now the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius, the Roman emperor. Pontius Pilate was governor over Judea; Herod Antipas was ruler over Galilee; his brother Philip was ruler over Iturea and Traconitis; Lysanias was ruler over Abilene. Annas and Caiaphas were the high priests. At this time a message from God came to John son of Zechariah, who was living in the wilderness. Then John went from place to place on both sides of the Jordan River, preaching that people should be baptized to show that they had repented of their sins and turned to God to be forgiven. Isaiah had spoken of John when he said, “He is a voice shouting in the wilderness, ‘Prepare the way for the LORD’s coming! Clear the road for him! The valleys will be filled, and the mountains and hills made level. The curves will be straightened, and the rough places made smooth. And then all people will see the salvation sent from God.’”
days.reflection: In our world today, we often think the most important people are the beautiful, the powerful, and the famous. They seem to get all the attention. They seem to have all the power and fame. Whether this is true in politics or just your class, everyone seems to follow the elites. This gets us to think that we are missing out if we don’t follow the demands they place on us. But when you look back through the course of time, it's amazing how people who seemed so important ended up becoming irrelevant. Celebrities die and are forgotten, heroes are buried in unmarked graves, the kingdoms of the powerful end in rubble. This was true in Jesus’ day too. Rome was the greatest empire in history. Caesar was the most powerful man alive. For the Jewish community, the high priest was someone super important, kind of like a modern-day pope. If you lived in the first century, you would have thought that these were the people that history would remember. But take a look at their names and ask yourself how much you know about each one. What do you know about… Tiberius, the Roman emperor? Pontius Pilate the governor of Judea? Herod Antipas the ruler over Galilee? Philip the ruler over Iturea and Traconitis? Lysanias the ruler of Abilene? Annas and Caiaphas the high priests? You probably don’t know a thing about them, except the verses you have read right now. Those who seemed important at the moment ended up being forgotten by history. In fact, they are only remembered by most people today because they are mentioned in the biblical story. Now, what do you know about John the Baptist? At this point, quite a lot. Yet where was John when the powerful elites were on the stage of history? He was in the wilderness. John wasn’t in the center of power. John wasn’t famous. Yet God overlooked Caesar, Herod, Pontius Pilate, and the high priest and bought his message to a man in the middle of nowhere. Take heart, young man, God doesn’t view history the way the world does. You don’t need to be famous; you don’t need power; you don’t have to live in an important place for God to use you. To be important in the eyes of God is to figure out the story he is telling in your day and age and to play your role in it. In the same way that God skipped over the power brokers of Rome to bring the word of the Lord to a man who cared, he will use you if your heart is available to him. Remember 2 Chronicles 16:9a? The eyes of the LORD search the whole earth in order to strengthen those whose hearts are fully committed to him. Your job is to prepare the way for the Lord, not try and be one. Your job is to make sure his message can get through. All you have to be is available and he will do more with you than you can comprehend. Jesus took John from the wilderness and fisherman from their nets and thrust them into history shaping the story of God. He can use you too if you are open. Are you?
questions: If you could be remembered for anything, what would it be and why?
Ask your Dad who the most famous people were when he was growing up. Get him to list a few of them out. How many of those people do you know? What does that teach you about fame?
What sort of characteristics in a young guy's life do you think draws God's attention to him?

thoughts.verse: This is what the LORD says: “Don’t let the wise boast in their wisdom, or the powerful boast in their power, or the rich boast in their riches. But those who wish to boast should boast in this alone: that they truly know me and understand that I am the LORD who demonstrates unfailing love and who brings justice and righteousness to the earth, and that I delight in these things. I, the LORD, have spoken!
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you see the world the way he does. Ask him to shift your perspective to value what he values.

days.title: Clearing The Path
days.scripture: LUKE 3:1-6 It was now the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius, the Roman emperor. Pontius Pilate was governor over Judea; Herod Antipas was ruler over Galilee; his brother Philip was ruler over Iturea and Traconitis; Lysanias was ruler over Abilene. Annas and Caiaphas were the high priests. At this time a message from God came to John son of Zechariah, who was living in the wilderness. Then John went from place to place on both sides of the Jordan River, preaching that people should be baptized to show that they had repented of their sins and turned to God to be forgiven. Isaiah had spoken of John when he said, “He is a voice shouting in the wilderness, ‘Prepare the way for the LORD’s coming! Clear the road for him! The valleys will be filled, and the mountains and hills made level. The curves will be straightened, and the rough places made smooth. And then all people will see the salvation sent from God.’”
days.reflection: If a road isn’t used very often, it can fall into disrepair. The concrete can erode and be washed away by storms, making it unsafe. Trees can fall across the road and block the path so you can’t get through. Rocks can slide off the mountain and fall into the middle of the road. There is nothing more frustrating than needing to go somewhere when the way is blocked. If you need to get somewhere and you can't get through, someone is going to have to go ahead to clear the path. For the people of God in the time of John the Baptist, the road to God has fallen into neglect and disrepair. Loyalty to God had been washed away with compromise. Idols had fallen on the highway of holiness. Human personalities and movements had blocked the way to God. In the ancient world, when someone important was coming to visit a city, say a king or a high-ranking official, they would commission forerunners to go ahead and make sure the road was smooth. They would alert people to the arrival of the king and make necessary repairs for a safe and efficient journey. John the Baptism was a forerunner. He was sent ahead by Jesus to clear the path back to God. Jesus was the king of the Jews coming to visit his people, and preparations were needed for when he showed up. John woke the people up from their slumber with urgent preaching. John got the culture ready by calling them back to God. John got people’s hearts ready by calling them to repent. Whenever God wants to move, he will send forerunners ahead to get things ready. Your generation hasn’t really seen God move. Lots of things are blocking people from coming to Jesus. Sin, fear, distractions, anxiety. So much stuff is getting in the way. Clearing the way is a job for men. Men care about clear pathways, but boys are happy to play where they are. Men use their strength and influence to make way for Jesus. Boys only think of themselves. You need to keep leaning into the shift of moving from the whole to a part. You need to keep reminding yourself that you are in God's story, not just your own. Then you can accept the responsibility as a man to play your part in the story he is telling. As a young man making this shift you can: Make a way for God with kindness when others are mean. Make a way for God by pushing away temptations. Make a way for God by turning your attention to what he may want to say when you are at youth group or church. Make a way for God by inviting others to youth group with you. There is a real sense of joy in clearing the way. You know you have done something important. You know you have done something useful. You know you have made a difference. Why not ask God to make you a forerunner in your generation?
questions: Can you think of any other famous men in history who have played the role of a forerunner like John the Baptist? Who were they, and who did they help prepare the way for?
What do you think are some of the things blocking people your age from encountering God? What sort of stuff seems to get in the way of them meeting Jesus?
Ask your Dad how God prepared his heart to receive Jesus into his life. Who prepared the way, shared the message, and helped get him ready? What happened when Jesus came in?

thoughts.verse: A lazy person’s way is blocked with briers, but the path of the upright is an open highway.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to remove anything in your heart that may be blocking you from experiencing God's love and grace. If you sense him saying anything specific, offer it to him and ask for his grace to take it away.

days.title: Washed Up
days.scripture: LUKE 3:1-6 At this time a message from God came to John son of Zechariah, who was living in the wilderness. Then John went from place to place on both sides of the Jordan River, preaching that people should be baptized to show that they had repented of their sins and turned to God to be forgiven. Isaiah had spoken of John when he said, “He is a voice shouting in the wilderness, ‘Prepare the way for the LORD’s coming! Clear the road for him! The valleys will be filled, and the mountains and hills made level. The curves will be straightened, and the rough places made smooth. And then all people will see the salvation sent from God.’”
days.reflection: Teenagers can get dirty. Guys can go for days without showering and end up smelling stank-y! Sometimes you can’t even tell until you come home and mom says, “You stink! Go take a shower.” Water is one of the only ways we can keep our bodies clean. Dirt and sweat and junk accumulate on us and no amount of deodorant or cologne can cover it up. You have to wash it off. The same can be true of sin in a person’s life. We can get involved in things we shouldn’t, be exposed to things that are wrong, or make poor decisions that hurt our relationship with God. You can also get contaminated by the stuff of the world. It can just happen so easily. No matter how much good we do to try and cover it up, it's still there and it still stinks. John knew people needed to be clean for the coming of someone as important as Jesus. No one wants to visit someone who hasn’t bothered to shower or get dressed up. So he called the people to be baptized. Baptism had a long history for God's people. In John's day, baptisms were a part of being ceremonially and ritually clean before God. These baptisms were called tevilah, and the point of them was to make sure you were ready to step into God's presence. John’s baptisms were a way of taking the well-known imagery of Old Testament ritual immersions and using it to share a new message. The Messiah is coming; wash up. They were a sign that God was doing something new, and people had to get the junk off their lives for a visit from the king. These baptisms didn’t make people right before God; they showed that people wanted to be made right with God. If you are going to be someone who lives well in the story of God, you are going to need to be clean. Not just physically (though that will help), but spiritually. You are going to need to have your sin, shame, and mess washed away by God's grace. This is the reason Jesus came. He came to cleanse us of our sins. 1 John 1:9 says. “if we confess our sins to him, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all wickedness” There is nothing you have done that God can't forgive and wash away. He can make you clean and fresh in his presence. He can take away any guilt or shame you may have. He can ease your conscience and restore your confidence before him. All you have to do is confess. He will forgive and cleanse.
questions: What is the dirtiest and smelliest you have ever gotten? What were you doing to get so covered with muck? How long did it take you to get clean?
The Bible promises to cleanse us from our sins. This sounds like a kind of bath. How do you think God cleanses us from our sins?
Ask your dad if he has been baptized. If he has, what lead him to make this decision, and how did he understand its meaning?

thoughts.verse: ave mercy on me, O God, because of your unfailing love. Because of your great compassion, blot out the stain of my sins. Wash me clean from my guilt. Purify me from my sin. For I recognize my rebellion; it haunts me day and night. Purify me from my sins, and I will be clean; wash me, and I will be whiter than snow.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment and still your heart before God. Is there anything you feel you need to confess to him? Take a moment and do that, and ask him to forgive you and cleanse you. Then accept his forgiveness and know that you are clean by his grace.

days.title: Raising Up And Tearing Down
days.scripture: LUKE 3:1-6 At this time a message from God came to John son of Zechariah, who was living in the wilderness. Then John went from place to place on both sides of the Jordan River, preaching that people should be baptized to show that they had repented of their sins and turned to God to be forgiven. Isaiah had spoken of John when he said, “He is a voice shouting in the wilderness, ‘Prepare the way for the LORD’s coming! Clear the road for him! The valleys will be filled, and the mountains and hills made level. The curves will be straightened, and the rough places made smooth. And then all people will see the salvation sent from God.’”
days.reflection: Have you ever thought about how the first pioneers traveled across America from East to West? They had to walk through every forest, cross every river on foot, climb every mountain, and descend through every valley. It would have been up, down, up, down, over thousands of miles. As technology advanced and society grew, they knew they were going to have to fix the roads in order to make travel faster, safer, and smoother. They blasted down the mountains with dynamite and used some of the rubble to fill in the valleys. They even blasted through the mountains in some spots and made tunnels. They raised up, tore down, over and over again so the smoothest road possible was made across the country. 200 years ago it would have taken you more than a year to cross America on foot. Today, you can drive across the country in about 47 hours nonstop. There is even a crazy cross-country race called the Cannonball Run to see who can do it in the fastest time. (Google it!) Turns out that blasting down mountains and filling in valleys is effective if you are trying to build a road to get somewhere fast. John the Baptist was a kind of spiritual road worker. Instead of tearing down mountains, he tore down idols. Instead of filling in valleys, he raised up what was missing in people’s relationships with God. He was committed to doing whatever it took to get people to God in as clear a way as possible. In your world today, there seem to be so many things lacking to help people find Jesus. There is a lack of awareness, love, and understanding that needs to be filled in. And there are mountains in the way making the journey to him hard. Mountains of hypocrisy, misunderstanding, and confusion. God wants to demolish the things getting in the way of you knowing him. God wants to build bridges over the doubts that stop you from coming to him. He wants to have as smooth a path as possible. God has a role for you to play in doing this for others too.
questions: What is the longest roundtrip you have ever taken? Where were you going and why?
Have you ever felt there were big things blocking your relationship with God? Things that felt like mountains in the way? If so, would you be willing to share about it now?

Ask your Dad about a mountain that was in the way of his relationship with God, and how God tore it down. Did he use another person to help in any way? If so, who was it, and what happened as a result?

thoughts.verse: Then Jesus said to the disciples, “Have faith in God. I tell you the truth, you can say to this mountain, ‘May you be lifted up and thrown into the sea,’ and it will happen. But you must really believe it will happen and have no doubt in your heart. I tell you, you can pray for anything, and if you believe that you’ve received it, it will be yours.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: If there is anything blocking your relationship with God right now, bring it before him and ask him to show you a way through it. Ask him to tear it down so you can journey farther in your relationship with him.

days.title: Fruit That Lasts
days.scripture: LUKE 3:10-14 The crowds asked, “What should we do?” John replied, “If you have two shirts, give one to the poor. If you have food, share it with those who are hungry.” Even corrupt tax collectors came to be baptized and asked, “Teacher, what should we do?” He replied, “Collect no more taxes than the government requires.” “What should we do?” asked some soldiers. John replied, “Don’t extort money or make false accusations. And be content with your pay.”
days.reflection: There are two kinds of change in the world. Shallow change and deep change. Shallow change means that you get excited about something new and make a bunch of promises, but when the excitement wears off, you go back to your old life. Deep change means that something has happened inside of you that causes you to think and act completely differently about something. It forms a new conviction that proves your motive was real. John the Baptist is preaching, preparing the way for Jesus, when the least likely people show up to be baptized. The crowds. John tells them to share what they have with the poor. Corrupt tax collectors. John tells them not to use their power to rip people off in their taxes. Roman soldiers. John tells the Roman soldier to use their power properly and not extort people. What's interesting about these things is how practical and applicable they were. John wanted to see deep change in the hearts of people, not shallow change. Lots of guys your age can get caught up in emotions at youth camps, retreats, and worship nights. They can get all excited and committed when things are fun, but they drop off or drift away. That’s because they experienced shallow change. But God desires deep change. Change of our heart, desires, and lives. This is not about going forward again and again at an altar call or trying harder to be good. It’s about tangibly adjusting our lives and priorities for Jesus to come in. This week, you have seen John the Baptist get the people ready for Jesus. He has cleared a pathway among the people for Jesus to appear. He has torn down the mountains and raised up the valleys to make a path for people to come to Jesus. He has called for deep change and tangible repentance for those getting caught up in the excitement, and now the stage is set for Jesus to appear. God is looking for forerunners today. Guys like you, who will take their place in the story of God and point people to Jesus. This is a huge shift compared to how other guys think, but you are making great progress. Be available, and history will be shaped.
questions: What are some of the ways you can tell that someone is truly sorry? How can you tell if someone didn’t really mean it?
If John the Baptist was to come to your school, what do you think he would say to the various groups of people?
Ask your Dad about when he learned about deep change. How did his heart and behavior both change as a result?

thoughts.verse: Prove by the way you live that you have repented of your sins and turned to God. Don’t just say to each other, ‘We’re safe, for we are descendants of Abraham.’ That means nothing, for I tell you, God can create children of Abraham from these very stones.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to continue to produce a deep change in you. Ask him for grace in any area you are struggling with right now.

extras: WEEK 5 MANSCHOOL ROAD TRIP MANSCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: SOMETHING TO SAY Learning how to develop a clear path is an important skill. This week, plan a road trip and have your son plan, navigate, and provide directions the whole way. NO APPS allowed. No google maps, no apple maps, no GPS, no turn-by-turn. Make the destination somewhere he has always wanted to go, but doesn’t know how to get by memory. You may need to order a Rand McNally or map it out on printed websites that you can follow along. Make sure the reward at the end of the trip is worth it. I recommend something about an hour away so it feels meaningful. A quick note. It’s important to talk him through how to do this. Gen Z are digital natives, and for the most part, don’t comprehend how to navigate life without digital resources. You may want to watch a couple of YouTube videos, dust off MapQuest, and walk him through it. The goal is fun and a challenge, not stress and failure. OPTION 2. WATCH Season 1, Episode 4 of The Chosen This is where John the Baptist first appears. The Chosen is probably the best movie account of the story of Jesus out there. Help your son realize JTB was not just a historical person, but an actual man with a personality, story, needs, desires and dreams. Seeing it on screen can sometimes help make it a touch more real and relevant.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
